column3: Which statistical test was used?
How was an LLM used?
column4:
Feature extractor, Hallucinations

The authors use a large language model to extract fine-grained attribute–opinion pairs from user reviews (e.g., “<Price, expensive>”, “<Quality, well-constructed>”). To overcome hallucinations, they first pick the most frequent, important attributes from e-commerce pages and then prompt the LLM only for opinions about those attributes.  These extracted pairs seed an attribute-specific user–opinion–item graph for each attribute.
column5:
Feature extractor

Traditional sequential recommenders rely on capturing implicit collaborating filtering signals among items. Later on, sequence aware recommenders were proposed, which incorporate explicit relationships among user preference items, where these explicit relations are modeled through knowledge graphs. 

In this paper, the authors propose to leverage the Large Language Model (LLM) to provide new types of relations and connections between items. Different from the previous methods which rely only on predefined relations among items. Particularly, the Large Language Model (LLM) is used to generate language knowledge representations of items based on their textual information such as titles, brands, and descriptions.

For this purpose, the authors employ GPT-3’s text-embedding-ada-002 model to convert item texts into dense semantic embeddings. These embeddings capture rich world knowledge and serve as inputs for discovering latent relations between items.

The LLM is not used for text generation or reasoning, but purely for embedding extraction.
Its outputs enhance the model’s ability to identify hidden item connections and improve sequential recommendation performance.
column6:
RL policy

Problems: LLM used to learn reinforcement policies, and inference latency
The paper adapts an LLM (Mistral-7B) as an offline environment (LE) that supplies both state and reward signals to RL-based recommenders. 

It uses parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA adapters) and introduces item tokenization to map item text into the LLM’s embedding space for efficient learning.

A reward model is built with an instruction prompt combining user history and a candidate item; a small score head turns the LLM’s final hidden state into a scalar reward.
column7:
Feature extraction, Inference latency


Prompts an LLM to produce per-item descriptive/preference knowledge, then encodes that text with a lightweight text encoder (e.g., BERT) into a compact vector. This vector is concatenated with the item-ID embedding and fed to a standard ID-based sequential recommender, so the model uses real-world knowledge, sequential patterns and collaborative signals. The LLM is then fine-tuned using feedback from the recommender to make the recommendations more useful and accurate.
column8:
Recommender

In this study, the authors have introduced a simple yet effective prompting strategy to extract the intent of the user from each session at the semantic level. The proposed prompting strategy consists of four modules: prompt initialization, optimization, and selection module to choose the best prompt by exploiting the robust generalizability of LLMs across diverse domains. The prompt initialization module instructs the LMM to predict the next immediate item by inferring the varying intent present in an ongoing session.
column9:
Recommender

They use the LLM (Llama2-7B) as a generative next-item selector: the user’s session history and a candidate set are written as an instruction prompt, and the LLM outputs one item from the candidates.

To make the LLM understand sequential behavior, they inject “behavioral tokens” (embeddings from a trained sequential recommender) into the prompt via a projector, creating a hybrid item representation (text token + behavioral token).

They instruction-tune the LLM with LoRA and use curriculum prompt tuning: start with text-only prompts (easy), then gradually shift to hybrid prompts (hard) so the LLM internalizes both world knowledge and recommender behavior patterns.
column10:
Reranker

This study examines LLMs’ tool-use abilities by casting the LLM as a central controller (“surrogate user”) in a recommender setting. The LLM reads the user’s context/history and, via a chain-of-thought process (Thought → Action → Observation), decides when to call two attribute-oriented tools: Retrieval (to pull candidate items from an item pool using attribute encoders) and Rank (to order candidates with an attribute-aware instruction). A lightweight memory strategy verifies and stores candidates across rounds, helping the controller refine choices and decide when to stop. Using in-context demonstrations, the system iteratively explores attributes and converges on a final recommendation list.
column11:
Recommender

In this study, the authors propose an efficient and effective lightweight framework for fine-tuning a pre-trained large language model on recommendation data, utilizing the LoRA adapter and instruction tuning based on few-shot training settings to generate accurate recommendations. For fine-tuning purposes, they transform a base LLaMA-7B into a Large Recommendation Language Model by lightweight, two-stage instruction tuning—first Alpaca instruction tuning, then rec-tuning on recommendation data—using LoRA.
column12:
Embedding generation, Computational cost

In this work, the authors propose an LLM-enhanced recommendation model that leverages retrieval to integrate LLM insights into recommendations. The proposed framework consists of two stages: (1) the authors first use an adaptive sampling strategy to find a representative and diverse set of users, who mirror the diverse preferences and behavioral patterns of the whole community. A varied and representative sample set is leveraged to create an LLM reasoning pool using the chain-of-thought prompting approach. During the second stage, to reduce computational cost for implementing it in industrial applications, the framework matches each user embedding with users in the LLM reasoning pool. Consequently, all users gain access to the open-world knowledge generated by LLM. Lastly, users and items embeddings are fed into traditional recommender systems to develop recommendations.
column13:
Recommender 

The authors propose a model named CALRec, a two-stage fine-tuning framework that fine-tunes a pre-trained LLM using two contrastive losses and a language loss. In the first round, the pre-trained LLM is fine-tuned on data from multiple domains, followed by a second round to further fine-tune on target domain data. They use pure text to represent each item and user-item interaction, and then pre-train an LLM on a large corpus of text for sequential recommendation. Technically, each item is flattened semantically, concatenating multiple attributes such as product name, title, category, brand, and other textual information. However, they discard non-semantic attributes such as item-id and user-id, which may add noise to the LLM input.  The primary objective of the authors in formulating the whole problem as text data is to exploit the strong potential of LLMs for understanding and providing reasoning for textual data.
column14:
Embedding generation, Inference latency

This study proposes DLLM2Rec, a model specifically tailored for knowledge distillation from a large language model (LLM) to a conventional sequential recommender. The approach adopts a teacher–student strategy and introduces three distillation weighting schemes that combine the rankings produced by the LLM and the conventional sequential model, while keeping inference latency low for real-time recommendations. In short, the goal is to leverage the LLM’s semantic reasoning while maintaining inference latency comparable to that of conventional sequential models.
column15:
Recommender, Hallucination 

The authors modify the KP4SR model that integrates item knowledge graphs with an LLM for generating sequential recommendations. The authors argue that existing conventional and LLM-based recommender systems utilize item knowledge but ignore user knowledge, which is critical for generating personalized recommendation lists. Therefore, they introduce user-knowledge graphs, which, in combination with item knowledge graphs, are input into LLM to generate a user-specific recommendation list. Additionally, the denoising prompts are designed to mitigate the hallucination issue associated with LLMs.
column16:
Recommender


The authors present TLRec, a transfer learning framework designed to enhance the performance of sequential recommendation tasks across domains. TLRec integrates both open-source models (e.g., GPT-4) and closed-source models (e.g., LLaMA-7B). The open-source model is employed to construct instruction-tuning data from the source domain using a chain-of-thought prompting strategy, while the closed-source model is fine-tuned on this instruction-tuning data. For fine-tuning, the authors adopt LoRA and curriculum learning strategies. Finally, the fine-tuned model is utilized to generate recommendations in the target domain.
column17:
Embedding generation, Computational cost of LLMs 


This paper tackles the joint video and comment recommendation problem, aiming to model user preference from both viewing and commenting perspectives. The proposed model integrates a sequential recommender system as a backbone with a supplement LLM model used in the alignment phase, but the LLM is discarded in the deployment phase to reduce computational cost. LLMs are used (i) as an offline text encoder for titles/comments and (ii) as a generative recommender driven by instruction templates to align semantics with the SR model.
column18:
Feature extractor
This study focuses on the joint modeling of search and recommendation (S&R). The authors point out that both search and recommendation can mutually benefit each other; however, there is a trade-off when a model handles them simultaneously, as they rely on different strategies. For instance, collaborative information is essential for recommendation, while semantic signals between queries and items are crucial for search to generate accurate ranking lists. Therefore, it becomes non-trivial to design an adaptive solution that balances consideration of both collaborative information and semantic signals for S&R.

Current models use a single representation per item, making it difficult to capture distinct patterns for search and recommendation. In this study, the authors adopt a large language model that assigns a unique identifier to each item, while also allowing multiple identifiers to capture both semantic and collaborative signals. The proposed model, named GenSAR, unifies balanced search and recommendation through generative retrieval. The model consists of two components: semantic codes, which represent semantic information, and collaborative codes, which preserve collaborative signals.
column19:
Feature extractor

In this study, the authors have designed a heterogeneous user modeling strategy named as HUM using the large language model to effectively capture users’ heterogeneous behaviors across multiple domains to tackle the problem of open domain recommendation (multi domain recommendation).  Existing studies, containing ID-based and semantic based strategies, struggle with poor generalization and lead to seesaw phenomenon in which these strategies optimize results for one domain while ignoring other domains. The proposed model consists of two main components: (1) compression enhancer which exclude noisy patterns and extract diverse representations by compressing user preferences across multiple domains. For this purpose, the special token is designed to retrieve heterogenous behaviors of a user, guided by compression prompt. Afterwards, users and items input are fed into an LLM for joint learning and used last hidden layers to represent users and item. To find relevance among users and items, the authors have leveraged inner dot product, and then item with highest relevance score considered for recommendation. 2) robustness enhancer module ensure that each domain is optimized without favoring to one specific domain, mitigating the domain seesaw phenomenon. In other words, it introduces domain importance score to balance the optimization of each domain.
column20:
Embedding generation


A novel model-agnostic LLM-based recommendation generalization framework was introduced to mitigate semantic bias in zero-shot cross-domain sequential recommendation. For this purpose, an LLM layer is integrated into conventional models, such as GRU4Rec and SesRec, which act as a feature encoder to generate transferable sequential features for the target domain. The integrated LLM layer operates at the item level and the semantic level. At the item level, it captures inter-domain compactness to ensure that the item embeddings are closely aligned across diverse domains. At the sequential level, it transfers behavioral sequential patterns through clustering and attention-based aggregation, maintaining the item-level diversity within the domain.
column21:
Recommender

While current sequential recommenders are effective in the form of sequence-to-sequence text, they exhibit three limitations:

they fail to capture intra-user explicit and implicit features from textual data such as titles and descriptions,

they underutilize cross-user collaborative filtering signals, and

they rely on ineffective strategies to fuse signals from the previous modules.

To address these limitations, the MoRE model is proposed, which consists of three coordinated modules:

extracting explicit features (e.g., product titles and descriptions) and implicit behavioral patterns (e.g., brand loyalty) from a user's interaction history,

leveraging a pretrained model to learn cross-behavioral patterns among users through the user–item interaction matrix, and

using a meta-reflector that dynamically selects optimal signals for each user through contextual bandit optimization by carefully analyzing explicit, implicit, and cross-behavioral patterns to generate a personalized recommendation list.
column22:
Recommender

Time intervals between purchasing items are a crucial factor in sequential recommendation tasks, whereas existing approaches focus on item sequences and often overlook by assuming the intervals between items are static. However, the dynamic interval acts as another dimension, which describes not only the user-profiling history of a user but also for other users who have a similar kind of history. In this study, the authors have a new model named IntervalLLM, which uses an attention-based mechanism to merge the information items and interval embeddings.  For this purpose, they have fine-tuned the Llama model using the item embedding and interval embeddings by instruction tuning.
column23:
Data augmentation, Embedding generation

GPT-3.5 generates extra user/item text by leveraging a user history, and then a text-embedding model (text-embedding-ada-002) turns that text into vectors. Afterwards, the core encoder is a LightGCN model, which fuses ID embeddings with embeddings learned by the LLM to generate a ranking list. Additionally, Visual signals are leveraged, encoded by the CLIP model.
column24:
Agentic AI, Recommender


In this paper, the LLMs act as the “brains” of user and item agents. Each user agent uses an LLM to read the user’s history, store a memory of their preferences, and then decide which item they would pick in a given situation. Item agents do the same from the item side, summarizing what kind of users like them. During training, the LLM is repeatedly prompted (selection + reflection) so these agents adjust their memories until their choices match real interaction data, and at test time the LLM-driven agents are used to rank items for each user. the reported results indicate that the proposed agentic based model prerformed diverse basline models.
column25:
Recommender: Overcome the computational overhead of LLMs by reducing the text size.

In this paper, LLMs are employed in two stages: first, as a summarizer, and then as a recommender. The interaction history of users is converted into text, then blocks, and an LLM-based prompt is designed to summarize these blocks into a compact summary. After this, the compact summary with a few user recent interactions and the description of candidate items is fed into another LLM, which is fine-tuned using LoRA to answer yes and no for each candidate item. The purpose of summarizing users’ long historical interactions is to overcome the computational overhead of LLM by reducing the text size.
column26:
Embedding generation


In this study, the authors propose a collaborative large language model for recommendation (CLLM4Rec), a generative recommender system that tightly couples the ID-based paradigm with the LLM-based paradigm. The authors argue that previous LLM-based approaches (P5, M6, TALLRec, etc.) rely on item IDs or the sequence of text descriptions for recommendations, which have significant issues. Therefore, the proposed CLLM4Rec has extended the pre-trained GPT-2 models using item_ids and user_ids vocabularies, and further trained them to learn contextual and collaborative signals from the content and user-item interaction matrix, respectively. During the training phase, the content LLM is trained on textual data and learns an embedding that is very close to a collaborative embedding. Similarly, the collaborative LLM retrieves an embedding that is closely aligned with the content embedding. Neither of these LLMs is equipped to make recommendations. Therefore, in the inference phase, another LLM-based module is fine-tuned on collaborative embeddings to generate a recommendation list for each user.
column27:
Feature extractor, Embedding generation


In this paper, the LLM is used as a backbone to extract features from user and item texts. Through instruction tuning, the model learns powerful hidden representations of users and items. These representations are then used by the model’s output layers to generate the final recommendation list.
column28:
Recommender


This paper proposes the TransRec model, which provides a way to leverage LLM as a recommender system by overcoming the gap between the language space and the item space. It represents each item through multi-facet identifiers (ID, title, attributes, etc.), so the LLM captures both distinctiveness and semantic patterns, instead of just focusing on ID or plaintext. For ground generation, it leverages the FM-index (a special data structure for fast searching) with beam search to generate a recommendation list from in-corpora items. Llama and BART language models are used for experiments, which are fine-tuned using instruction data.
column29:
Embedding generation

In this study, the A-LLMRec model is proposed, which uses an LLM as a backbone to generate recommendations. In the first stage, it leverages collaborative signals obtained from pre-trained collaborative models and text embeddings acquired from pre-trained BERT models. Both types of embeddings are aligned using an encoder to capture collaborative and textual knowledge in a shared representation. In the second stage, the shared embeddings and user embeddings are inserted into a prompt, along with the user’s history and candidate items, to obtain a recommendation for the next intended item.
column30:
Recommender, Overcome training and inference computational overhead.


Large language models (LLMs) have shown promising results in the recommendation research area. However, their practical use is constrained by long training and inference times because these models rely on billions of parameters to process recommendation data, which makes them difficult to retrain on real-time data. In this paper, the LSC4Rec model is introduced, which aims to integrate the benefits of both an LLM and small recommendation models (e.g., DIN, GRU4Rec, SASRec). The LLM component (P5) is trained on historical interaction data formatted as text, while the small recommendation model is trained on user behaviour data. At inference time, the candidate ranking list generated by the LLM and the small recommendation model are fused to improve recommendation performance. With this strategy, the LLM does not need to be retrained again and again; instead, only the small model needs frequent updates.
column31:
Embedding generation

This paper presents an LLM-based feature encoder, which is fine-tuned on a user-interaction sequence comprising textual patterns to facilitate the learning of collaborative filtering signals. Subsequently, it is employed as a backbone model to generate embeddings of user-item sequences, which are then incorporated into conventional sequential models for downstream recommendation tasks.
column32:
Recommender 

In this paper, the authors argue that existing LLM4Rec models fail to capture the sequential signals inherent in user-item sequences. Therefore, they conduct multiple experiments by shuffling user-item sequences during both training and inference phases. The results highlight the limitations of LLM4Rec models in capturing sequential patterns. To address these limitations, a simple yet effective LLM-based model called LLM4-SRec is proposed. The model transfers sequential user representations from a pre-trained CF-SRec (e.g., SesRec) into an LLM-based model, which then directly produces user-item representations to generate the next immediate recommended item.
column33:
Recommender

In this paper, the authors use DeBERTa V3 base as the LLM backbone to model user histories and candidate items as text. For each source domain, they fine-tune DeBERTa V3 using LoRA adapters, enabling the LLM models to learn/get the domain-specific knowledge without modifying the main weights. In the target domain, they do not leverage a fresh LLM for recommendations; instead, they mix and reweight all hidden states obtained from fine-tuned LLM models on source domains. Lastly, these hidden states are used to make final recommendations for the target domain.
column34:
Embedding generation, Inference latency, cold start problem


This paper presents a framework with three phases: pre-training, alignment, and disentangling to enhance sequential recommendations. In the initial phase, a traditional sequential model (e.g., SeSRec) and an LLM model (e.g., Llama3-8B) are pre-trained on recommendation data. During the alignment phase, the authors introduce an alignment loss that combines a characteristic alignment loss with a Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) loss based on the recommendation labels. The alignment loss ensures that all statistical properties of the distribution are considered during the alignment. In the final phase, an LLM expert and a recommendation expert are used to fuse embeddings from the conventional and LLM models to generate the recommendation list.
column35:
Recommender



In this paper, the LLM (LLaMA2-7B) is fine-tuned with instructions to take a user’s interaction history, emotion labels extracted from their reviews, and a small set of candidate items as input, then rank and recommend the best items. It doesn’t work alone: a RoBERTa-based sentiment model first turns reviews into sequential sentiment labels, and a SASRec model provides candidate items, while the LLM learns to combine this behavioral and emotional context into personalized recommendations.
column36:
Recommender, Cold start problem


Conventional models encode textual data as numerical values, which prevents learning the common-sense knowledge and semantic patterns within the data. In this paper, the authors directly input textual data into an LLM model by designing a question-answering template. The LLM model (e.g., Llama-2-7b-longlora-32k) was then fine-tuned using a newly created prompt-based question-answering dataset to produce more accurate point-of-interest recommendations. Each prompt includes a current trajectory block, a historical trajectory block, an instruction block, and a target block for each user.
column37:
Embedding generation, Hallucination

In this paper, the LLM (Llama-2-7B) is used to read a user’s POI visit history written as natural-language “instructions.” Each POI is converted into a textual description (city, district, street, category, time), and these sequences are fed into the LLM to capture semantic and sequential patterns. Additionally, the authors have employed a traditional model (LightGCN) to extract collaborative signals, which are then combined with the LLM embeddings to create unified embeddings that incorporate both semantic and collaborative signals. After that, the model is fine-tuned by leveraging unified embeddings and the user’s instructions for the next-POI prediction task.
column38:
Summarizer and Embedding generation. 

In this study, the authors present a modality-aware point-of-interest recommendation model that utilizes DeepSeek-R1 and GPT-4o for summarizing unstructured text data and photos, respectively. The generated summaries are then fed into OpenAI’s text-embedding-ada-002 model to produce modality-aware embeddings, which are subsequently passed to the BERT4Rec model to generate recommendations for the next point of interest.
column39:
Summary generator, Embedding generation

In this study, the authors introduce the EmbSum model for content-based recommendations. The model includes User Poly-embedding and Content Poly-embedding modules to measure the relevance score between users and candidate items. However, the authors argue that simply concatenating users' and items' history sequences is insufficient to fully capture user engagement. Therefore, Mixtral-8x22B is used to summarize a user’s historical sequences, which are then assessed using GPT-4 as a judge. Finally, the generated summaries are embedded using a pre-trained P5 model to combine them with User and Item embeddings.
column40:
Embedding generation, Data augmentation


In this paper, the authors investigate the potential of enhancing content-based recommendations by utilizing open- and closed-source large language models (LLMs). For each type of LLM, they designed different strategies. For the open-source model, a discriminative recommendation approach is designed, where the LLM model (e.g., LLaMA-7B and LLaMA-13B) serves as a text encoder and is further fine-tuned using LoRA to enhance the performance of the downstream recommendation task. For a closed-source model like GPT-3.5-turbo, which has more than a billion parameters and is adept at capturing semantic patterns, the authors designed prompting strategies to augment the context and user’s features, which contribute to improving the performance more compared to open-source LLM models.
column41: LLM is used as a conversational recommender (CRS)—i.e., it generates dialogue responses and recommendations —and the paper evaluates how it behaves compared to humans.
column42:
LLM as a retriever and response generator. 

In this study, the authors propose a framework that uses the LLM-based GRITLM-7B model to create a chat-based recommender system. The LLM functions as a recommender system for two main tasks. In the first task, the LLM acts as a retriever to obtain a list of relevant candidate items. Additionally, it retrieves a list of relevant users to understand collaborative patterns among users. In the second task, the LLM acts as a dialogue management system to decide whether to continue the conversation; if so, it ranks the relevant candidate items and generates the next response. To accomplish these tasks, the authors have optimized the model to improve its text embeddings for retrieval and have also jointly fine-tuned it for response generation using a prompting and parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy.
column43:
LLM as a response generator, evaluator, and emotion capturer


To bridge the gap between system outputs and user needs, the authors proposed an ECR framework to introduce empathy into conversational recommender systems (CRSs). The framework utilizes multiple LLMs at various stages to construct an empathetic CRS. GPT-3.5-turbo is first used to annotate emotions in user utterances, and these labels are then used to fine-tune a GPT-2 model that automatically classifies emotions for all dialogues. These emotion signals guide an emotion-aware recommendation module that consists of Llama 2–Chat, which is fine-tuned with emotional reviews and retrieval-augmented prompts to generate rich, empathetic responses. In the last stage, GPT-4 turbo serves as an evaluator to score for the emotional, logical, and informative quality of the generated replies. Moreover, the authors have introduced a new metric to tailor to user satisfaction in real-world CRS scenarios.
column44:
Embedding generation


In this paper, the authors use Llama2-7B as the main backbone LLM and keep its weights frozen.
They first “reindex” the vocabulary by asking the LLM to generate synthetic language–to–item and language–to–reason corpora, distilling recommendation signals into special item tokens.
Then they “adapt” the LLM by training a small aggregator and gating modules on top of its hidden states to predict items, making the overall approach lightweight and parameter-efficient.
column45:
Embedding generation


In this study, the authors propose the Large Vision-Language Conversational Recommendation Framework (LaViC). The model uses a large language model in two stages: (1) visual knowledge self-distillation, which compresses the product image from thousands of pixels into a small set of tokens to lower computational costs while preserving the image's integrity, and (2) a fine-tuning process that refines the language model by incorporating both dialogue context and distilled visual tokens. In this way, the LLM acts as a conversational recommender that jointly leverages both textual and visual information to produce recommendations.
column46:
Data pruning strategy to reduce computational cost, LLM as a recommender, 


In this work, the authors present a data pruning technique called DEALRec for more efficient LLM-based recommendations. The approach identifies the most influential data samples and then fine-tunes the LLM models using a few-shot strategy, significantly reducing computational costs while maintaining or even enhancing accuracy. To achieve this, DEALRec balances two objectives: high accuracy, which leads to the selection of highly representative samples, thereby reducing the empirical risk, and high efficiency, which indicates the fine-tuning of LLMs on representative samples instead of full tuning. The method computes an influence score to estimate each sample’s impact on model performance using a small surrogate model (e.g., SASRec), and introduces an effort score to capture how difficult each sample is for the LLM to learn. Together, these scores allow the method to select representative and LLM-significant samples for efficient fine-tuning.
column47:
Explanation generator, Classifier


In this paper, the LLaMA-3-7B model with LoRA adapters is used to produce natural-language explanations for why a user might like or dislike a target item, providing a chain-of-thought prompt based on the user's history of liked and disliked items. These explanations are then used in two ways: first, the same LLM is prompted as a CTR predictor (Yes/No) to determine whether the explanation aligns with user behavior (LC alignment); second, the explanations are encoded into vectors and used as additional features for an ID-based CTR model (IC alignment). The LLM is fine-tuned through reinforcement learning using these two rewards to ensure its explanations align with user preferences to enhance CTR prediction.
column48:
Feature extractor, Recommender


The model first utilizes Vicuna-13B as a feature extractor to transform every item into a semantic embedding, and for each user–target pair, it retrieves the top-K most similar past items (SUBR). It then builds a prompt containing the user profile, the user’s recent behavior sequence, and a description of the target item, ending with a yes/no question about whether the user will like it. Vicuna-13B reads this prompt and its “Yes” vs “No” logits are interpreted as the predicted CTR, so the LLM itself acts as the recommender. In the few-shot setting, the same LLM is lightly fine-tuned with LoRA on a mix of original and top-K most similar past items, so it learns to use the retrieved behaviors more effectively when making predictions.
column49:
Reranker

In this study, the GPT-3.5-Turbo and GPT-4-Turbo models are used as rerankers. For this purpose, the authors designed a one-shot prompt containing a query and a list of candidate items and asked the LLM to rerank the list of candidates based on the given query.
column50:
Data augmentation
News recommender systems heavily rely on textual information from news articles, which makes them vulnerable to textual attacks that can manipulate the ranking and exposure of articles. In this study, the authors introduce LANCE, an LLM-based news content rewriting framework designed to influence the rankings of published news. The framework consists of two components: an explorer and a reflector. First, the explorer uses diverse prompts (writing styles, personas, sentiments) to rewrite the titles and abstracts of original articles and then evaluates these rewrites with a news recommender system, identifying both successful and unsuccessful variants based on ranking changes. Using these labeled rewrites, the reflector fine-tunes an open LLM on instruction-style triplets containing the original text, an effective rewrite, and a failed rewrite. After fine-tuning, LANCE can generate new, effective versions of articles that are used to carry out targeted textual attacks on news recommender systems.
column51:
Embedding generation


In the proposed model, the authors use LLMs in two main roles. First, ChatGPT (gpt-3.5-turbo) is prompted to generate theme-level “topic” representations for each news article and theme-level user profiles from clicked history; these LLM-generated themes are then combined with semantic text representations to form dual-level embeddings used by standard news recommenders for ranking and filtering. In the second role, the second LLM (LLaMA-7B) is used as the generation model. Its purpose is to produce a single coherent “multi-news narrative” from a set of related news articles, so the system can recommend news together with an interest-aware story rather than only ranking items.
column52:
Feature extractor, personalized recommendation


The paper uses an LLM as a semantic helper/feature extractor. It enhances news categories per article in the MIND dataset, extracts entities, and clusters them into similar interest groups to build an entity graph. Additionally, it generates a textual user-interest profile from click history, which is encoded into user representations. The authors compare LLaMA2-7B vs. ChatGPT (gpt-3.5-turbo) and report stronger results with ChatGPT.
column53:
Recommender


The paper uses an LLM (e.g., GPT-3.5) as a non-trained generator to produce both product bundles and natural-language “shopping intents” from a user’s session. In the proposed framework, the authors employ retrieval-augmented generation to retrieve related sessions of the target session, and then design zero-shot and few-shot prompting strategies to bundle generation and identify the current intent in the target session.
column54:
Recommender, Explanation generator.


In this paper, the authors use public LLMs as the group recommender and the explanation generator. They create synthetic data and prompt an LLM to generate a bundle recommendation with an explanation.
column55: Measure fairness in LLM-generated recommendations across sensitive and non-sensitive attributes. The authors leveraged both closed- and open-source LLMs and used zero-shot prompting to generate recommendations.
column56:
Embedding generation, classifier, Oercome the computational compexity 


ColdLLM avoids running the LLM on every user by using a two-step pipeline. First, it extracts LLM-based content embeddings for users (from their clicked-item contents) and for cold items (from item content), then performs a fast similarity search to select only the top-K candidate users for each cold item. Next, the LLM is run only on this small candidate set with a Yes/No prompt to decide which users would interact with the cold item. Those predicted “Yes” interactions are treated as pseudo-clicks and are used to update the cold item embedding in the recommender training pipeline.
column57:
Recommender, Proposes a mechanism to control diversity in LLM-generated recommendations

The paper uses a pretrained Llama3 model as the backbone and adapts it to recommendation via parameter-efficient instruction tuning (PEFT). They reformulate recommendation into three text-based sub-tasks—behavior pattern extraction, preference simulation, and sequence generation—and fine-tune the model on prompts built from user interaction histories plus genre metadata. During inference, the fine-tuned LLM generates recommendation sequences, while the proposed diversity-control module helps balance relevance with item coverage.
column58:
Reranker

LLM used as a reranker to control fairness, diversity, and accuracy in a recommendation list generated by another traditional model
column59:
Recommender

The paper uses ChatGPT as a recommender to generate top-K (K=20) recommendation lists for neutral and sensitive-attribute prompts (music and movie domains), and then quantifies fairness by measuring how much the lists change across sensitive attributes using multiple similarity- and fairness-based metrics.
column60:
Explanation generator


The paper uses an LLM mainly as a language generator to produce user-facing explanations, not as a standalone recommender. It first retrieves and compresses graph-based evidence (relevant paths/subgraphs) to fit the LLM context, then fine-tunes the model to generate explanations grounded in that retrieved evidence. The LLM’s outputs are evaluated with automatic semantic metrics (e.g., BERTScore/BARTScore) and a small human study to assess explanation quality and stability.
column61:
Explainer

In this paper, the LLM is not the main recommender like SASRec. Instead, it is trained as a surrogate model to mimic the predictions of a fixed target recommender (e.g., SASRec). After learning to imitate the target model’s outputs, the LLM is used as an explainer, generating natural-language reasons for why certain items are recommended.
column62:
Evaluator


Multiple open and close source LLM models used an evaluator to measure the quality of the generated explanations of items.


In this paper, the authors investigated the feasibility of utilizing LLMs as evaluators for recommendation explanation texts. We leverage real user feedback as ground-truth labels to validate the quality of LLM evaluation
column63:
Recommender 



The paper uses an LLM as a planner that decides what to recommend next based on a user’s interaction history, aiming to maximize long-term reward instead of only short-term accuracy. It is trained with reinforcement learning (PPO) in simulated environments so the LLM learns better actions over multiple steps. The model also keeps a small reflection memory (retrieving top-K reflections, e.g., K=2) and feeds it back into the prompt to guide future recommendations.
column64:
Teacher/refiner, computational overhead, latency inference. 


The paper uses an LLM (e.g., DeepSeek-R1) to rewrite or refine the slate produced by an RL slate recommender, using the user’s session history and candidate items to select a better 10-item list. Calling an LLM at serving or prediction time is computationally expensive. Therefore, the LLM model is used during training but ignored at prediction time. To use it only during training, the authors added a regularization loss that aligns the RL actor’s actions with the LLM-refined actions, so the final model behaves like the LLM without needing the LLM at inference.
column65:
Recommender, explanation generator.


They use LLMs (GPT-3.5 Turbo, LLaMA2-7B-chat, and Mistral-7B-Instruct) as instruction-following recommenders: given a user’s liked items plus item metadata, the model generates a ranked top-K list and an explanation in natural language.
column66:
Data augmentation, Embedding generation


The paper uses an LLM as a user-behavior simulator: given a cold-start user’s small source-domain history and a target item’s text metadata (e.g., title + attributes), it predicts whether the user would interact with that item. These simulated interactions are then used to augment the sparse target-domain data and train a conventional recommender (instead of using the LLM directly for ranking at prediction time).
column67:
Evaluater


The paper uses an LLM (Mistral 7B) as a pre-training preference judge: given a user’s history and a small set of candidate items, it is prompted to pick the most preferred item (or “None”).  That LLM choice is converted into a simple reward signal (1 if an item is selected, 0 if “None”), creating an LLM-generated offline interaction dataset used to train an actor–critic RL policy. After trainig, they stop using the LLM online and instead fine-tune/adapt the pre-trained RL policy with feedback from the (simulated) user environment.
